top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 100, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Suzuka Endurance Road's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before July.
Suzuka Endurance Road's mixed terrain rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
Suzuka Endurance Road's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 100 miles.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what Suzuka Endurance Road's mixed terrain actually does to you.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 100 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
2-minute all-out efforts on mixed terrain. Closing gaps, responding to attacks, sprinting for position. Train the burn.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Suzuka Endurance Road. Train it until it feels boring.
100 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 100 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Suzuka Endurance Road, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Suzuka Endurance Road’s 100 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 100 miles of mixed terrain in Suzuka Circuit, Mie Prefecture, Japan.
